In addition to much tree damage from straight line winds, there was one tornado in Connecticut, 2 in Rhode Island, and 1 in Massachusetts. These all occurred in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hartford, Arlington St at Natick St was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary remained over the area which produced multiple rounds of torrential rainfall form heavy thunderstorms from July 9th through July 10th. CAPE values of 2500 to 3500 J/kg and PWAT values of near 1.8 allowed heavy thunderstorm development to produce rainfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour at times. There were parts of the area that receive anywhere from 3-5 inches of rain in a several hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three cars submerged near Bank Street and Broad Street due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the area forced torrential thunderstorms in a tropical airmass resulting in widespread flash flooding in CT. The first round of thunderstorms occurred early in the morning on July 16th. Another round of thunderstorms occurred later in the afternoon. The tropical airmass had CAPE values 1500 - 2000 J/kg with PWATs 2.2-2.4 inches. The torrential thunderstorms produced rainfall rates as much as 3 inches an hour. Rainfall amounts totaled a widespread 2-4 inches across Connecticut with localized spots seeing as much as 5 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding taking place in neighborhoods on the southside of New Haven with high standing water with some impassible roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over the area provided a focus for convection to develop in the afternoon on July 4th. CAPE values of greater than 2000 J/kg and PWAT values near 1.8-2 allowed thunderstorms that developed along the stationary front to slowly drift southward while producing torrential rainfall rates of 1-2 per hour. Some areas saw 1-3 of rain over a 1 to 3 hour period which produced localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water halfway up several cars in an apartment complex parking lot near Dove Lane.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several Metro North Railroad underpasses impassible. Several roads closed due to flooding including Cutspring Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow brought elevated dewpoints in the 70s to southern New England leading to a heat advisory. All the heat and humidity led to good instability which, along with forcing from a strong mid level jet produced severe storms in Massachusetts and Connecticut and some flash flooding in Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Storrs 1 inch hail was reported at the University of Connecticut.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 with maximum winds of 100 mph began on Bailey Hill Road |in the town of Killingly, CT between Cranberry Road and Ledge |Road, here the tornado removed shingles from a two story home and |either snapped or uprooted healthy and mature trees. The tornado |moved northeast towards Ledge Road causing similar tree damage |before dissipating on Shippie Schoolhouse Road in the town of |Foster, RI. Tree damage along the path was consistent with wind |speeds between 90 and 100 mph, resulting an EF-1 rating on the |Enhanced Fujita Scale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep longwave trough, south-southwest flow at all levels, and the development of a QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) created all the ingredients for a widespread and susbstantial flash flood event across much of southern New England.  For a few hours during the late morning, helicity values were high and a brief tornado formed along the QLCS in North Brookfield, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Glastonbury, a tree and wires were down on Naubuc Avenue.  Report relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid atmosphere existed over Connecticut, with a little short-wave energy. That was enough to trigger an isolated severe thunderstorm in Hartford County late in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Burlington, a tree and wires were down on Route 4 between Rt 179 and River Rd in Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system emerged from the southern U.S. into the Northeast resulting in mixed precipitation over northwestern Connecticut Wednesday (January 25th) into Thursday (January 26th). Mixed precipitation over the area resulted in school closures and slippery roads across parts of northwestern Connecticut.

A potent mid level low/shortwave deamplified as it moved east-southeast from the Ohio Valley late on the 27th and early on the 28th.  At the surface, while the parent low lifted across the Great Lakes, a secondary low developed over DelMarVa in response to height falls from approaching trough...then moved east, passing well south of southern New England.  The result was a northwest-to-southeast oriented band of heavy snow from western MA to northern and eastern CT to southern RI.  Six to nine inches of snow fell in those regions.  There was a very sharp cutoff to the northeast, with only one to two inches falling in northeastern MA and Cape Cod.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hartford County generally received 4 to 8 inches of snow.  Some specific amounts included:  8.0 inches in Granby; 7.0 inches in both North Canton and Simsbury; 6.8 inches in Bristol; 6.5 inches in East Windsor; 6.2 inches in Newington; 6.0 inches in Glastonbury; 5.9 inches a Bradley International Airport in Windsor Locks; 5.0 inches in Marlborough; and 4.0 inches in Wethersfield.

A brief but powerful arctic airmass moved across the region on February 3-4, 2023 which brought dangerously low wind chill values to much of the region along with strong wind gusts. An arctic cold front passed across the region during the early morning hours on February 3 accompanied by a few snow showers. Thereafter, very cold air gradually advected into the region on February 3 into the morning hours on February 4.||Air temperatures gradually fell throughout the day on February 3 with readings falling to 5 to 15 degrees below zero by the early morning hours on February 4.||In addition to the cold, this arctic air mass brought strong winds to the region during the daytime hours on February 3 into the overnight hours before easing on February 4. Peak wind gusts between 30 and 40 mph were observed. These winds resulted in at least some downed tree limbs and branches.||The combination of very cold air and strong winds resulted in wind chill values much lower than the air temperature. The lowest wind chills ranged from 20 to 40 degrees below zero. For many, the last time if was this cold was in February of 2016.||Warming centers opened to offer assistance to those needing to shelter from the cold.||Key impacts: minor tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary remained over the area which produced multiple rounds of torrential rainfall form heavy thunderstorms from July 9th through July 10th. CAPE values of 2500 to 3500 J/kg and PWAT values of near 1.8 allowed heavy thunderstorm development to produce rainfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour at times. There were parts of the area that receive anywhere from 3-5 inches of rain in a several hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads are closed in urban areas of New London including Broad St, Bank St, Garfield Ave, and Shaw St. Spotter reports 2 inches of rain in 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the area forced torrential thunderstorms in a tropical airmass resulting in widespread flash flooding in CT. The first round of thunderstorms occurred early in the morning on July 16th. Another round of thunderstorms occurred later in the afternoon. The tropical airmass had CAPE values 1500 - 2000 J/kg with PWATs 2.2-2.4 inches. The torrential thunderstorms produced rainfall rates as much as 3 inches an hour. Rainfall amounts totaled a widespread 2-4 inches across Connecticut with localized spots seeing as much as 5 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bank Street impassible at the intersection of Shaw Street due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near-record heat and a strong short-wave trough digging southeastward across Quebec led to the development of afternoon thunderstorms, mainly along the higher terrain in central MA and northeast CT.  A few of these storms produced isolated wind damage and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 0.75 inch diameter hail in South Coventry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak high pressure and a marginally cold air mass was locked into the northeast with blocking to the north across Eastern Canada, while low pressure over the Midwest moved east and redeveloped off the Northern Mid Atlantic coast. This led to the first widespread accumulating snowfall across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the northern half of the county. Snowfall reports from trained spotters of 6.8 inches at 1 E Danbury, and 6.3 inches in New Fairfield were reported.  Also a Cocorahs observer reported 6.2 inches at 2.4 NNE Ridgefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak high pressure and a marginally cold air mass was locked into the northeast with blocking to the north across Eastern Canada, while low pressure over the Midwest moved east and redeveloped off the Northern Mid Atlantic coast. This led to the first widespread accumulating snowfall across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across much of the southern half of the county, especially further west. A trained spotter 6.4 inches in Weston, and 6.0 inches in Fairfield, and a Cocorahs observer reported 6.1 inches in Norwalk, with a public report of 6.0 inches at 4 SSE Easton and 5.5 inches at 2 SW Bridgeport.

The following snowfall reports came in just after sunrise as temperatures were marginal which likely led to some compaction just before the measurements were taken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic Air from a 1040+ mb high pressure system built into the region from the west providing strong N to NW winds and sub zero temperatures to NE portions of Southern Connecticut. An arctic cold front passed through the region during the morning of February 3, 2023. Strong cold air advection along with a tightening pressure gradient produced strong winds in an arctic air mass across Southern Connecticut during the evening and a good portion of the overnight into the early morning on February 4, 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station KCTEASTH159 in East Haddam, CT reported a wind chill of -30 F beginning at 1:54 am. Also, mesonet station KCTDEEPR2 in Deep River, CT reported a wind chill of -27 F beginning at 3:09 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high amplitude, negatively tilted trough moving across the Great Lakes and into New England along with cold and warm front provided strong forcing which resulted in flooding, wind damage, and 5 tornadoes. In the warm sector dewpoints surged into the mid 70s with PWATs near 2 inches which led to torrential downpours. Tornadoes occurred in Scotland, Ct, Scituate, RI, North Attleboro, MA, Stoughton, MA, and Weymouth, MA. The tornadoes ranged in intensity from EF-0 to EF-2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Southington, the northbound lane of Rt 322 was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system, stacked vertically from the surface through the upper levels, was located over the Gulf of Maine and the Canadian Maritimes. An upper level disturbance rotating around this low moved southeastward from Quebec to New England, triggering scattered afternoon thunderstorms in southern New England, a few of which were strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Stafford, CT near the Monson, MA border, quarter size hail was reported, per amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure meandered just off the southeast coast of New England from late on the 13th to early on the 15th, before moving eastward and farther offshore.  In its wake, it produced 2 to 3+ feet in northern and western MA.  Lesser amounts of 6-12 were common over interior lower elevations.  In eastern CT, RI and eastern MA, a coating to 2 inches were common, as precipitation type was mainly wet snow and rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals varied widely from as much as 18 in the northwest hills of Hartford county to as little as 1.5 inches in the southeast section of the county.  Some specific totals include - 18.0 in Granby/Hartland, 12.5 in Burlington, 7.2 Suffield, 5.1 in Bristol, 4.5 at Bradley International Airport in Windsor Locks, 3.0 in Glastonbury and 1.5 in Manchester.  In East Hartland, at 220 pm on the 14th, multiple trees and powerlines were down with several roads impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong upper low and surface high overhead resulted in a major cold snap across northwestern Connecticut the night of May 17th into the morning of May 18th. Strong radiational cooling from clear skies resulted in widespread overnight low temperatures in the mid 20s to lower 30s. This resulted in significant impacts to crops/vegetation across the area. A disaster declaration was made for northwestern Connecticut due to the extreme cold and crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Note: Damage estimates will be determined in the Fall of 2023 based on actual crop harvest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Nor'Easter resulted in widespread moderate to heavy wet snow and gusty winds particularly across northern Litchfield County in northwestern Connecticut March 13-March 15, 2023. Anywhere from 6 to 15 inches fell across the area. This resulted in multiple downed trees and powerlines which resulted in multiple power outages across the region.||Key Impacts: Power Outages.

A powerful Nor'Easter resulted in widespread moderate to heavy wet snow and gusty winds particularly across northern Litchfield County in northwestern Connecticut March 13-March 15, 2023. Anywhere from 6 to 15 inches fell across the area. This resulted in multiple downed trees and powerlines which resulted in multiple power outages across the region.||Key Impacts: Power Outages.

An anomalously strong cold snap for mid May standards possibly resulted in some crop damage as temperatures overnight Monday morning of May 15th dropped into the 30s across northwestern Connecticut. The coldest locales saw overnight low temperatures fall into the low to mid 30s. These temperatures were conducive for frost formation across parts of Litchfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Note: Damage estimates will be determined in the Fall of 2023 based on actual crop harvest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Miller B type storm brought rain which changed over to wet snow across Interior Southern CT. The heaviest snow totals were mostly driven by elevation across far northwestern Fairfield as temperatures were right at freezing. Most places closer to the coast had considerably less snow, which was mainly on colder surfaces. Locations to the east across the interior had accumulating snow but amounts were below warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported snowfall varied greatly along the western edge of Northern Fairfield. Most of the zone received snowfall under 6 inches going east and south.  The higher elevations reported over 6 inches of snow along the far western portions of the zone as temperatures were marginal and snow reports were strongly based on elevation. A trained spotter reported 7.5 inches at 4 WNW Fairfield. Broadcast media reported 7.0 inches at 3 NW Ridgefield at a higher elevation. The remaining reports all came in well below 6 inches. Cocorahs reports included 2.7 inches in Redding, 2.0 inches in Shelton, 1.9 inches in Bethel and 1.5 inches in New Canaan and Sandy Hook.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs reports came with 3.4 inches in Naugatuck and 2.7 inches at 1.2 WSW Seymour. Broadcast media reported 3.8 inches at 4 ENE Naugatuck, with the public reporteding 3.3 inches in Southbury and a public reported of 7.0 inches at 1 NNW Oxford.  Reports along the southern eastern portion of the zone at lower elevations were under 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough as a cold front approached at the surface. Just before a line of thunderstorms reached our area along the cold front, thunderstorms developed over our area in a zone of low-level warm advection. This moisture eventually interacted with the storms along the cold front and helped strengthen them. Severe thunderstorms developed and produced damaging winds in southern CT.

Damage consisted of large limbs broken off two trees as most of the path was over open field. The tornado started along Minor Bridge Road and moved northwestward and uphill before ending near Town Line Road. Maximum winds were estimated at 80 mph with a path length of 0.75 miles and a width of 30 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow brought elevated dewpoints in the 70s to southern New England leading to a heat advisory. All the heat and humidity led to good instability which, along with forcing from a strong mid level jet produced severe storms in Massachusetts and Connecticut and some flash flooding in Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Enfield, a tree and wires were down on School St and Nonotuck St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep longwave trough, south-southwest flow at all levels, and the development of a QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) created all the ingredients for a widespread and susbstantial flash flood event across much of southern New England.  For a few hours during the late morning, helicity values were high and a brief tornado formed along the QLCS in North Brookfield, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Rocky Hill, a large tree was uprooted, per picture on social media.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over the area provided a focus for convection to develop in the afternoon on July 4th. CAPE values of greater than 2000 J/kg and PWAT values near 1.8-2 allowed thunderstorms that developed along the stationary front to slowly drift southward while producing torrential rainfall rates of 1-2 per hour. Some areas saw 1-3 of rain over a 1 to 3 hour period which produced localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to ten impassible roads from high water. A few homes flooded in Ansonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved through aloft as a broken line of severe thunderstorms developed along a surface trough. The severe storms produced damaging winds across southern CT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch tree limbs broken and shingles blown off in Bridgeport CT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorms developed in an unstable and moist environment on September 11th, 2023 and produced flash flooding in Connecticut. Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front that was draped over the area in an environment with 2000-2500 J/kg of SBCAPE and 1.7-1.9 inches of PWAT. The thunderstorms produced rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour and a total of 1-3 inches of rainfall in a couple of hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swath of Mill Plain Road closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough extending from a low pressure system over the Western Atlantic provided a focus for persistent heavy convective rain to continually develop over the NYC metro and surrounding areas during much of the day on September 29th, 2023. In an environment with anomalously high moisture given the time of year, PWATs near 1.5-1.7 inches, rounds of heavy rainfall fell over the same areas for more than 12-18 hours. While the rainfall rates were generally 1 inch per hour or less, the persistence in the heavier rainfall resulted in a widespread total of 4-6 inches of rain with JFK receiving almost 9 inches of rain from the event. There were some brief periods of time where rainfall rates peaked at near 2 inches per hour. This resulted in widespread flash flooding across Southeast NY and Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Street at Montgomery Street closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level system brought widespread rounds of showers and thunderstorms to northwestern Connecticut on July 9-10, 2023. Northern and western portions of the county were hardest hit with rainfall amounts between 4 and 7 inches. This resulted in numerous reports of flash flooding and washed out roads and culverts.||A local declaration of emergency was issued in the Town of Norfolk following flash flood damage along Route 272. The damage along this road caused some homes to become isolated. A State of Emergency was also issued by Goshen First Selectman Todd Carusillo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two houses were evacuated along Preston Mountain Road due to rising flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms associated with a shortwave trough and cold front developed and moved over parts of northwestern Connecticut during the afternoon and evening hours on July 27th resulting in multiple reports of downed trees and wires across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was down on the road across Riverside Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked up the East Coast and intensified in the process as it brought widespread heavy rainfall across northwestern Connecticut on December 18, 2023. The rain fell light to moderate in intensity during the early morning hours, then became heavier during the later morning and early afternoon hours before tapering off. Rainfall totals were between 3 and 5 inches. This rain led to both areal and river flooding across the region. River flooding was primarily concentrated along the Housatonic as it took until December 21 for flood waters to recede along some portions of the river.||In addition, wind gusts between 30 and 40 mph combined with the soft ground surfaces resulted in downed trees and branches which hit some wires causing power outages. Some of the communities impacted included Torrington and Winsted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over portions of Saw Mill Hill Road.

River flooding was primarily concentrated along the Housatonic as it took until December 21 for flood waters to recede along some portions of the river.||In addition, wind gusts between 30 and 40 mph combined with the soft ground surfaces resulted in downed trees and branches which hit some wires causing power outages. Some of the communities impacted included Torrington and Winsted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Housatonic River at Stevenson, Connecticut rose above flood stage during the early morning hours on December 18 and peaked at moderate flood stage (maximum height of 16.49 feet) from the late morning hours on December 18 into the overnight hours on December 19. The river fell below flood stage during the morning hours on December 21. At this height, numerous homes in the Maples area along Indian Well Road in the City of Shelton were affected.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Housatonic River at Gaylordsville, Connecticut rose above flood stage during the morning hours on December 18 and peaked at moderate flood stage (maximum height of 10.26 feet) during the early morning hours on December 19 through the afternoon hours on December 19. The river fell below flood stage during the evening hours on December 21. At this height, water reached portions of West Street, Young's Field Road and Spring Street in the Town of New Milford. In addition, US Route 7 at Bridge Street flooded in the Town of New Milford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of gusty winds and soft ground surfaces resulted in reports of downed trees and wires resulting in some power outages. Torrington and Winsted were among some of the communities affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front ahead of a developing coastal low pressure system positions itself over the area late on December 17th and into December 18th. As the low pressure intensified and approached, significant amounts of moisture focused along the warm front ahead of the coastal low resulting in widespread areas of heavy rainfall that occurred over a 12-18 hour period. PWATs were 1.5-1.7 inches which were near record values. A widespread 2-5 inches of rain fell over the area. This resulted in many areas of flash flooding as well as significant rises in rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 63 closed in both directions due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north along the U.S. east coast and passed through the region during the morning of December 18, 2023. The pressure gradient tightened as the center of the storm approached, producing high winds over Southern Connecticut during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 66 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located in Stamford, CT at 458 AM Local Standard Time. A measured wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located in Fairfield, CT at 455 AM Local Standard Time. In addition, the Department of Highways reported a tree down on Route 15 northbound between exits 35 and 36 in New Canaan, CT , closing the right lane at 714 AM Local Standard Time.
